content:
7 min read
Just now

--

From my time at Microsoft scaling enterprise solutions, I have seen firsthand how new tools promise high velocity. AI-assisted coding tools like GitHub Copilot are no different, offering an undeniable boost to developer speed. This velocity is a fundamental customer truth. But this rapid output introduces massive engineering challenges, chief among them the accumulation of hidden technical debt. The core issue is that while tools can accelerate coding, they do not eliminate the need for sound architectural oversight. Many leaders are now facing the reality of Copilot technical debt and asking how developers can manage technical debt from AI-assisted coding.
Senior engineers can scaffold new services in minutes, and junior developers can unblock themselves faster than ever before. This initial acceleration feels like a significant gain, directly affecting sprint goals and feature delivery timelines. Yet, as a VP of Product, my focus also extends to our ‘platform constraints’: the long-term health and maintainability of our codebase. Understanding the risks of using AI coding tools like Copilot is crucial for sustainable growth.
The boilerplate code generated by these tools, while often functionally correct, rarely aligns with the nuanced architectural patterns we have spent years cultivating. It knows what to write, but not why we have made specific design choices. This gap is a primary way AI-generated code contributes to technical debt.
Here, I will explain why this initial velocity can mask this significant debt, leading to what I call ‘structural degradation.’ This is not a theoretical problem; it is a subtle tax on future development. Each accepted suggestion that bypasses our established API contracts or service boundaries adds a small, almost invisible crack in our foundation. Over time, these cracks compound, turning a clean, scalable system into a brittle one that slows us down later.
This creates a fundamental tension that every tech leader must now manage. Here is a visualization of how that degradation can manifest.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Architectural drift caused by unmanaged AI code generationArchitectural drift from AI boilerplate
When I was building scalable services at Meta, we consistently grappled with maintaining architectural integrity across rapidly expanding teams. A new engineer, no matter how talented, needed time to internalize the system’s design philosophy. AI suggestions lack this crucial context, introducing what I call architectural drift. It is the gradual, quiet deviation from your intended system design caused by localized, context-free optimizations. In my experience, this structural drift is the single biggest threat that automated coding assistants pose to long-term system health. The AI suggests a direct database call where a service layer abstraction is required, or it re-implements a utility function because it is unaware one already exists in a shared library.
Note: Architectural drift is insidious because no single code suggestion seems harmful. The damage accumulates slowly, making it difficult to spot until you are facing a major refactoring effort.
It is the ‘Reasoning-Complexity Trade-off’ in action: more capable AI models can generate incredibly complex logic, but they cannot yet reason about the second-order effects of that logic on the broader system. This often results in ‘bloated and coupled code,’ exacerbating existing ‘maintainability challenges.’ This leads to more than just suboptimal code; it contributes to a gradual, often imperceptible, ‘architectural decay’ that undermines our fundamental API thinking. Suddenly, services are intertwined in ways we never intended, and the cost of change escalates dramatically.
This drift is the primary mechanism through which AI introduces long-term technical debt. A clear framework helps quantify these indicators.
Technical Debt Indicators in AI-Assisted Development
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Measuring the true impact on maintainability
At Databricks, instrumentation was paramount for understanding product health; we did not make decisions without data. The same rigor applies to measuring technical debt in AI-assisted development. To truly manage AI-generated debt, we need data-informed roadmaps, not just gut feelings from our most senior engineers. Anecdotes about “messy code” are useful, but they do not provide the leverage needed to prioritize architectural work over new features. I insist on tracking quantifiable metrics to build a clear business case for investing in code quality and improving AI-generated code maintainability.
What is our ‘code churn’: how often do we touch, refactor, or delete AI-generated code within a few sprints of its creation? High churn suggests the initial “velocity” was an illusion. What is our ‘rework rate’ for these specific sections? And critically, what is the cognitive debt our engineering teams are accumulating? To understand cognitive debt in the context of AI-assisted development, it can be proxied by measuring the time it takes for a new engineer to understand a module heavily influenced by AI suggestions. This is a key aspect of systems design and a critical question for how AI code generation affects software maintainability.
Practical tip: Use commit message tagging (e.g., “[AI-Gen]”) to programmatically track the life cycle and churn rate of code originating from AI assistants.
These numbers provide the crisp decision records we need to assess our platform’s long-term health and make informed trade-offs between speed and sustainability. This leads to a more structured way of identifying problem areas and implementing robust review processes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI-Assisted code review workflowEstablishing AI-specific quality gates
This is not about halting innovation or banning helpful tools. Rather, it demands pragmatic leadership and the establishment of smart guardrails. These are key strategies to prevent AI-induced technical debt. This is central to effective AI coding tools code review and broader AI coding tools and code quality assurance. The answer to how AI-specific quality gates can prevent technical debt lies in automated checks in our CI/CD pipeline designed to catch the specific anti-patterns common in AI-generated code. This means going beyond standard linters and integrating advanced ‘static analysis’ tools that can enforce architectural rules. For instance, a tool like SonarQube can be configured with custom rules to detect when a generated function bypasses a designated service layer.
Watch out: Do not let your quality gates become a source of friction. The goal is to provide fast, actionable feedback to the developer, not to block them. Tune your rules to focus on high-severity architectural violations.
My experience has taught me that these quality gates turn what could be a one-way door of accumulating debt into a more reversible choice. By catching architectural deviations early, we allow our teams to leverage AI for speed without unknowingly committing to a future of expensive refactoring. This approach embodies a core principle: we want to ship and learn faster, but not at the cost of our system’s integrity. These automated checks ensure that every piece of code, regardless of its origin, adheres to our core API principles and architectural vision.
This structured review process helps us maintain a layered defense against debt, guided by clear decision-making.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI tool adoption decision record templateMy perspective on an AI-driven future
As a VP of Product, my role is to navigate the inherent tension between delivering customer value rapidly and ensuring the long-term sustainability of our platform. This is a classic ‘customer truth vs. platform constraint’ dilemma, amplified in the age of AI. Developer velocity is a customer truth and a competitive advantage. However, as a platform constraint, velocity without discipline can lead to a system that eventually grinds to a halt under its own weight. My pragmatic leadership approach involves embracing AI’s capabilities while acknowledging and actively managing technical debt from AI-generated code.
Practical tip: Regularly schedule “architectural deep-dive” sessions where you review modules with high AI-generated code churn to identify recurring anti-patterns and update your quality gates.
We must implement ‘trade-offs at scale,’ making conscious decisions about where to accept short-term velocity gains and where to enforce strict architectural purity. This requires asking what strategies can balance rapid development with code sustainability and having a bias toward ‘shipping, instrumentation, and learning.’ The future of much of software development, especially in areas like WebDev, is undeniably AI-assisted, but its success hinges on our ability to manage the associated cognitive debt in AI-assisted coding and maintain architectural integrity. It is about building a culture where every engineer is empowered by AI but also accountable for the long-term health of the codebase, making smart, data-informed decisions every step of the way.
It is our responsibility as leaders to equip them with the tools and frameworks to do so.
